claim: Current agents fail in real-world tasks because evaluating agents on static benchmarks is fundamentally different from evaluating language models
actionable: Build multi-dimensional agent benchmarks that measure both accuracy and cost; test agents in virtual environments that mirror real conditions
source_title: Building and evaluating AI Agents — Sayash Kapoor, AI Snake Oil

claim: Agent cost can balloon unexpectedly; without cost as a first-class metric, you lose visibility into agent efficiency and feasibility
actionable: Always measure tokens per successful outcome alongside accuracy; use Pareto frontiers to visualize cost-quality tradeoffs
source_title: Building and evaluating AI Agents — Sayash Kapoor, AI Snake Oil

claim: Static benchmarks don't predict real-world agent performance; Devon (coding agent) achieved 40% benchmark success but only 3/20 real-world tasks
actionable: Supplement benchmark-driven evaluation with real-world validation; involve domain experts to edit evaluation criteria proactively
source_title: Building and evaluating AI Agents — Sayash Kapoor, AI Snake Oil

claim: Capability (pass@K for high K) and reliability (99.999% consistency) are different; moving from 90% to 99.9% reliability requires systems thinking, not just ML
actionable: Focus on reliability engineering patterns: unit tests, verifiers, gradual rollouts, monitoring; treat agents as stochastic components needing system design
source_title: Building and evaluating AI Agents — Sayash Kapoor, AI Snake Oil

claim: Verifiers can have false positives in unit tests (e.g., human eval, MBPP); this causes inference scaling curves to bend downward with more retries
actionable: Audit verifier test suites for false positives; implement consensus across multiple independent verifiers for critical decisions
source_title: Building and evaluating AI Agents — Sayash Kapoor, AI Snake Oil

claim: Claimed agent capabilities (e.g., DoNotPay's legal automation, Sakana's scientific research) often fail rigorous evaluation and misrepresent real performance
actionable: Rigorously test claims against simplified, reproducible benchmarks before marketing; be transparent about failure modes
source_title: Building and evaluating AI Agents — Sayash Kapoor, AI Snake Oil

claim: Jevons Paradox: as LLM costs drop, overall agent infrastructure costs increase due to wider adoption and more intensive use patterns
actionable: Continue to optimize cost even as per-token pricing drops; implement budgets and resource limits; monitor total cost of ownership
source_title: Building and evaluating AI Agents — Sayash Kapoor, AI Snake Oil

claim: Agent evaluation must include human domain experts in the loop to validate and refine evaluation criteria continuously
actionable: Build evaluation workflows where experts proactively validate model outputs and adjust rubrics based on failures; avoid fully automated evaluation
source_title: Building and evaluating AI Agents — Sayash Kapoor, AI Snake Oil
